event: Pre-ceramic Amerindian peoples, sometimes called the Archaic or Ortoiroid culture, are believed to have first settled Anguilla as one of the earliest occupied islands of the Lesser Antilles.
Arawakan-speaking Saladoid peoples migrate from South America and establish ceramic-using agricultural settlements across Anguilla.
Indigenous inhabitants create ceremonial sites such as the Big Spring and Fountain Cavern, carving petroglyphs and using caves for religious worship of deities.
Taino society on the island, which inhabitants reportedly called Malliouhana, reaches its developed pre-Columbian form with villages, fishing, and cassava cultivation.

Christopher Columbus passes through the northern Lesser Antilles on his second voyage, after which Europeans came to know the elongated island; its later name 'Anguilla' derives from the word for eel, reflecting its shape.
A French expedition led by René de Laudonnière records sighting and noting the island during European exploration of the region.
The Dutch reportedly build a fort on Anguilla, marking an early European attempt at a presence on the island.
English settlers from Saint Kitts colonize Anguilla, beginning permanent European settlement and tobacco cultivation.
Carib raiders attack the fledgling English colony, destroying settlements and killing or driving off many settlers.
French forces from a regional expedition occupy and ravage Anguilla during conflict between England and France in the Caribbean.
Irish settlers arrive on Anguilla, adding to the island's growing population of European colonists and indentured laborers.
Many Anguillian settlers flee to nearby Antigua to escape renewed French raids during war between England and France.
Anguillians repel a French invasion force attempting to seize the island during the War of the Austrian Succession.
A French naval squadron raids Anguilla, burning settlements before being driven off with help from a British warship during the French Revolutionary Wars.
Anguilla is administratively placed under the control of nearby Saint Kitts, beginning a long and resented political union.
Slavery is abolished across the British Empire, emancipating the enslaved African population that had labored on Anguilla's struggling plantations.
The apprenticeship system ends and former slaves become fully free, though poor soil and drought leave the island's economy precarious.
Anguilla is incorporated into the British federal colony of the Leeward Islands, governed jointly with Saint Kitts and Nevis.
Anguillians petition Britain to be governed directly from London rather than through Saint Kitts, an early expression of separatist sentiment.
Anguilla is formally united with Saint Kitts (and later Nevis) into a single colonial presidency, deepening discontent among Anguillians.
Saint Kitts-Nevis-Anguilla joins the West Indies Federation as part of the British colonial reorganization of the Caribbean.
The West Indies Federation collapses, and Anguilla remains tied to Saint Kitts and Nevis under continued British administration.
Saint Kitts-Nevis-Anguilla becomes an Associated State with internal self-government, prompting Anguilla to rebel and expel the Kittitian police force in the Anguillian Revolution.
Anguillians vote overwhelmingly in a referendum to secede from Saint Kitts and form an interim governing council led by Ronald Webster.
Anguilla declares itself an independent republic, prompting Britain to land a force of paratroopers and London police in 'Operation Sheepskin' to restore order.
The Anguilla Act passes in Britain, formally separating Anguilla from Saint Kitts and Nevis and placing it under direct British administration with a commissioner.
Anguilla receives its own constitution, establishing a House of Assembly and a ministerial system of government.
Anguilla is formally and legally separated from Saint Kitts and Nevis, becoming a distinct British dependent territory.
A new constitution comes into force, granting Anguilla greater internal self-government with an elected chief minister.
Saint Kitts and Nevis becomes independent without Anguilla, confirming Anguilla's enduring status as a British territory.
Hurricane Luis strikes Anguilla, causing extensive damage to homes, infrastructure, and the developing tourism sector.
Hurricane Lenny brings unusual westerly winds and severe flooding to Anguilla's coastline.
The British Overseas Territories Act grants full British citizenship to Anguillians, reaffirming their ties to the United Kingdom.
A revised constitution introduces reforms expanding Anguilla's self-governance and the role of its elected legislature.
The global financial crisis hurts Anguilla's tourism- and finance-dependent economy, slowing development and revenue.
Hurricane Irma, a catastrophic Category 5 storm, devastates Anguilla, destroying buildings, the hospital, schools, and tourism facilities.
Anguilla begins earning major revenue from its '.ai' internet domain as demand for artificial intelligence branding surges worldwide.
The COVID-19 pandemic forces Anguilla to close its borders, sharply curtailing the tourism that drives its economy.
Booming registrations of '.ai' domain names bring tens of millions of dollars in annual revenue, transforming Anguilla's public finances.
